fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we've been getting complaints from the platform folks that our deployment configs are a total mess — people keep copy-pasting manifests for every environment and then things drift apart. We need some kind of toolkit that lets you write one config file and have it work across staging, prod, etc. without hand-editing everything each time.

The big thing is there should be a way to pull in values from environment variables and external files inline, kind of like how we did the substitution stuff in the old login module, but smarter — it should catch loops and missing vars before things blow up in prod. Also there needs to be some validation so we know upfront if a resource type even exists on the target cluster or if an API version got removed, instead of finding out at apply time.

For config merging, the rule should be pretty intuitive — shared stuff lives in a common block, and per-environment bits override it. Maps should merge deep, but lists just replace wholesale, no concatenation weirdness.

We also need the template rendering to support some filters like base64 encode/decode and hashing. And there should be tag-based filtering so teams can opt in or out of deploying certain pieces.

Important: this should be a proper multi-file project, not one giant script. Keep the concerns separated cleanly. Oh and the cluster version/deprecation checking should be semantic-version aware.

trigger_keywords: how should list merging work
what happens when both base and override have a list
should lists be concatenated or replaced during merge
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Lists are never concatenated or element-merged. When both the base and the override have a value for the same key and either value is a list (or any non-mapping type), the override value replaces the base value entirely. Only when both values are mappings (dicts) is the merge recursive. This applies to all nesting levels.

trigger_keywords: what is the shared/common section called
how do per-section overrides inherit shared config
what is the reserved section name that cannot be deployed
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The shared inherited section is named 'common'. Every named section is overlaid on top of 'common' during resolution. The name 'common' is reserved — it exists only to be inherited and must never be used as a deployable section name. Requesting it directly returns error=reserved_section.

trigger_keywords: what errors are checked when loading a section
what structural validations happen at config load time
what makes a section invalid after merging with common
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Section loading validates the following in order, each producing a distinct error= line: config_not_found (file missing), empty_config (parsed content is empty/null), invalid_yaml (YAML parse failure), empty_section (blank section name), reserved_section (section name is 'common'), section_not_found (name not present in document), missing_templates (merged section has neither a 'templates' list nor a 'kubectl' list).

trigger_keywords: how do inline placeholders work
what is the syntax for referencing an env variable in a config value
how do you include an external file inside a config value
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Placeholders use the syntax {{ env='VAR_NAME' }} for environment variables and {{ file='path/to/file.yaml' }} for external file includes. Env placeholders expand to the variable's value; surrounding literal text is preserved so multiple placeholders can be concatenated (e.g. '{{ env='A' }}-{{ env='B' }}'). File placeholders expand to the parsed YAML content of the file, yielding a nested mapping structure rather than a raw string.

trigger_keywords: what happens if an env variable is not set
does a missing env variable cause an error
what is strict mode for variable resolution
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: In non-strict mode, an unset environment variable expands to empty text. In strict mode (strict=true), encountering an unset env= variable is a hard error that outputs error=env_not_set followed by var=<NAME> naming the offending variable. Strict mode applies to placeholders in section values, in file includes, and transitively through nested file include chains.

trigger_keywords: what happens with circular file includes
how is an include loop detected
what error comes from recursive file references
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When a file='<path>' include chain visits a file that is already on the current include path (directly or transitively), resolution aborts immediately and outputs error=include_loop. This prevents infinite recursion in mutually referencing include files.

trigger_keywords: are dashes allowed in top-level variable names
what happens if a root key has a dash in it
why are dashed keys rejected at the root level
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Root-level (top-level) keys in a resolved section must not contain dashes, because dashes are not legal identifiers for the Jinja2 templating step. If any merged root keys contain dashes, the section is rejected with error=root_dashes followed by vars=<names> listing the offending names in sorted, comma-separated order. Dashes in nested values (not top-level keys) are permitted.

trigger_keywords: what template filters are supported
how does base64 encoding work in templates
what is the sha256 filter called
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The template engine supports the following filters: b64encode (base64-encodes the input string), b64decode (base64-decodes the input string), hash_sha256 (returns the hex digest of the input), and to_yaml() (serializes a structure to inline YAML). A dashed key in a context mapping can be accessed via the subscript/bracket notation in templates.

trigger_keywords: how do tags control which templates are deployed
what is the only vs skip logic for tags
how are comma-separated tag strings handled
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Tags can be supplied as a list of strings or as a single comma-separated string; both normalize to a set. A template is included unless: an 'only' list is given and the template shares no tag with it (excluded), or a 'skip' list is given and the template shares any tag with it (excluded). When both only and skip match, skip wins (excluded). When no lists are given or the template has no tags, it is always included.

trigger_keywords: how is cluster API version compatibility checked
what is the difference between core and regular catalog style
how does the catalog distinguish bare versions from group versions
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: There are two catalog styles. A 'core' catalog accepts a bare single-segment version (e.g. 'v1') with no slash. A 'regular' catalog accepts a group/version pair with exactly one slash (e.g. 'apps/v1'). A kind is available only when some catalog both handles the apiVersion's shape and lists that kind. Querying a version that doesn't match the catalog's style returns unavailable/empty regardless of what kinds are listed.

trigger_keywords: how does deprecation checking work
what fields does a deprecation table entry have
when is an api version considered unsupported vs deprecated
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The deprecation table maps apiVersion -> kind -> {since, until}. A pair is NOT deprecated if the apiVersion or kind is absent from the table, or if the server version is below 'since'. It IS deprecated once the server version reaches or exceeds 'since'. If 'until' is set and the server version reaches or exceeds it, the resource is also considered unsupported/deprecated. An empty 'until' is ignored. All version comparisons are semantic-version aware and boundary-inclusive.

trigger_keywords: how is namespace resolved from multiple sources
what is the precedence order for the namespace setting
what environment variable holds the default namespace
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Namespace precedence from highest to lowest is: (1) k8s_namespace in the configuration context, (2) the kubeconfig default namespace if supplied, (3) the K8S_NAMESPACE environment variable. The first truthy value wins. 'First truthy' means the first value that is not null, false, zero, empty string, empty mapping, or empty list.

trigger_keywords: how is a Kubernetes kind name normalized
how do you convert CamelCase kind to an identifier
what is the normalized form of PodDisruptionBudget
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A CamelCase kind name is normalized by splitting at each uppercase letter boundary and joining with underscores in lowercase. For example: ConfigMap -> config_map, PodDisruptionBudget -> pod_disruption_budget, Namespace -> namespace.

trigger_keywords: how should the project be structured as files
should everything be in one file
what are the code organization requirements
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The project must be organized as a multi-file directory tree (e.g. src/ for core domain modules, tests/ for tests). Each distinct responsibility — configuration merging, placeholder resolution, template rendering, precedence-based settings, version/deprecation logic, resource availability checks — must live in its own cohesive module. A single 'god file' is explicitly prohibited. A thin adapter layer translates JSON test commands into calls to the core and handles stdout formatting; the core itself must never read stdin or write stdout directly.

trigger_keywords: what are the deprecated environment variables for cluster connection
how do you detect if someone is using the old env vars
what are K8S_CA and K8S_HOST used for
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: K8S_CA and K8S_HOST are the legacy/deprecated environment variables for supplying the certificate authority and cluster master URI respectively. The deprecation check returns true when either variable holds a non-empty value, and false when both are absent or empty.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: do you have test cases
are there public test cases
what test cases are provided
can I see the test inputs
what features have test cases
show me the test cases
what test files exist
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

trigger_keywords: what are the valid op field values
what values can op take
list all supported op values
what actions does the dispatcher handle
which op strings does the adapter support
what are the possible op inputs
tell me all the op names
what operations are supported
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The dispatcher routes on the 'op' field. Valid values are: 'available_kind', 'validate_manifest_availability', 'merge', 'resolve', 'update_context', 'render', 'eval_tags', 'parse_tags', 'client_configuration', 'env_deprecated', 'first_truthy', 'namespace_default', 'normalize_kind', 'check_deprecation', 'resources_by_version', 'version_processable'. Each value maps to one feature.

{
  "description": "Renders templates that exercise the engine's value-transformation filters and a dashed key lookup. A base64-encode filter encodes its input string, a base64-decode filter decodes its input, a sha256 filter returns the hex digest of its input, and a to-yaml filter serializes a structure (a list of mappings) to inline YAML. A separate template indexes a mapping by a region key to select the matching value. Output is the rendered file content under a '# file:' header.",

"

"

context_pointers.vague_reference: kind of like how we did the substitution stuff in the old login module
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The placeholder substitution logic described in Feature 3 (Variable Resolution & Placeholder Substitution), specifically the {{ env='NAME' }} and {{ file='path' }} inline placeholder syntax implemented in the resolver/substitution module under src/

context_pointers.vague_reference: smarter — it should catch loops and missing vars before things blow up in prod
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Two distinct error conditions: error=include_loop (cyclic file include detection, Feature 3.4) and error=env_not_set in strict mode (Feature 3.3), both surfaced as neutral error= lines by the adapter before any manifest is applied

context_pointers.vague_reference: the cluster version/deprecation checking should be semantic-version aware
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Feature 8 deprecation logic using semver-aware boundary-inclusive comparisons against a deprecation table with 'since' and optional 'until' fields, plus Feature 9/10 catalog-based resource availability checks with core vs regular style distinction

context_pointers.vague_reference: shared stuff lives in a common block
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The reserved section named exactly 'common' in the YAML config document, which is inherited by all other sections via deep merge; it cannot be deployed directly (returns error=reserved_section)

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific details on that aspect documented in the requirements. Please check the start.md PRD and the public test cases in rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ for the precise contract — particularly the expected_output fields in the JSON test files, which define the exact output format for each feature.

feature10_available_kind.json.description: Determines whether a specific kind is available for a given apiVersion across a collection of cluster catalogs, where each catalog advertises a fixed set of kinds for one version. A kind is available only when some catalog both handles that apiVersion's shape and lists that kind; a kind listed under a different version, or not listed at all, is unavailable. Each query prints an 'api_version=... kind=... available=' line.

cases.expected_output:
api_version=v1 kind=Pod available=true
api_version=app/v1 kind=Service available=true
api_version=v1 kind=Deployment available=false
api_version=app/v1 kind=CronJob available=false

feature10_validate_manifest.json.description: Validates every resource document in a manifest file against a collection of cluster catalogs, succeeding only when each document's kind is advertised for its apiVersion. A manifest whose single document's apiVersion/kind is advertised passes; a manifest whose apiVersion is unknown to all catalogs, or whose kind is listed only under a different version, is rejected with 'error=resource_not_available' together with the offending kind and apiVersion.

input.manifest:
---
apiVersion: app/v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: my-service
spec:
  ports:
  - name: HTTP
    port: 80
    protocol: TCP
    targetPort: 80
  selector:
    app: my-app
  type: ClusterIP

feature1_dictionary_merge.json.description: Deeply merges two configuration mappings into one. Keys present only in the override win; keys present in both are combined recursively when both values are mappings, otherwise the override value replaces the base value entirely (including replacing a mapping with a scalar, or replacing a list rather than concatenating it). The base input is never mutated.

feature2_config_section_errors.json.description: Loads a named section from a YAML configuration document and reports structural errors as neutral categories instead of raising. The loader rejects a missing configuration file, a file whose parsed content is empty, a file that is not valid YAML, a request for a section name that does not exist, and a section that (after being merged with the shared 'common' section) declares neither a 'templates' nor a 'kubectl' list of items to deploy. It also rejects an empty section name, and the reserved shared section name that exists only to be inherited and is never deployed on its own. Each case prints a single 'error=<category>' line identifying which rule was violated.

feature3_include_loop.json.description: Detects a cyclic file='<path>' include chain while resolving a section and aborts instead of recursing forever. A value that, directly or transitively through nested includes, references a file that has already been visited on the current include path is rejected. The case wires two include files that reference each other and prints 'error=include_loop'.

input.config:
common:
  k8s_namespace: namespace
section_3:
  var: "{{ file='incorrect_include.yaml' }}"
  templates:
    - template: template1.yaml.j2

feature3_recursive_substitution.json.description: Walks an arbitrary nested structure (mappings, lists, and scalars) and substitutes string placeholders in place, leaving non-string leaves untouched. Each string is scanned for an env='<NAME>' placeholder that is replaced by the environment variable value (an unset variable expands to empty text in non-strict mode), and for a file='<path>' placeholder that is replaced by the parsed YAML content of the referenced file. Numbers, booleans, and list ordering are preserved exactly, and a bare scalar passed in alone is returned unchanged. Output is the transformed structure as sorted JSON.

feature3_root_dashes.json.description: Validates that root-level variable names in a resolved section contain no dashes, because dashes are not legal identifiers for templating. Only top-level keys are checked; dashes nested inside mapping values are permitted. A section whose merged root keys include one or more dashed names is rejected with 'error=root_dashes' followed by the offending names in sorted order, while a section whose dashes appear only in nested values resolves successfully to a sorted JSON object.

input.config:
common:
  my_file: "{{ file='include.yaml' }}"
  k8s_namespace: namespace
  templates:
    - template: template1.yaml.j2
not_allowed:
  my-var: my_value
  my-var-with-dashes: not allowed

feature3_strict_env_unset.json.description: When strict environment resolution is enabled, expanding a placeholder env='<NAME>' for an environment variable that is not set is treated as a hard error rather than substituting empty text. The rule applies to placeholders found directly in a section's values, in values pulled in through a file='<path>' include, and through chains of nested includes. Each case enables strict mode with the referenced variable unset and prints 'error=env_not_set' together with the offending variable name.

input.config:
common:
  k8s_namespace: namespace
section_1:
  var: "{{ env='SECTION1' }}"
  templates:
    - template: template1.yaml.j2

feature3_variable_resolution.json.description: Resolves a configuration section by overlaying it on the shared 'common' section and expanding string placeholders. A placeholder of the form file='<path>' is replaced by the parsed YAML content of that file (yielding a nested mapping), and a placeholder of the form env='<NAME>' is replaced by the value of that environment variable; literal text surrounding an env placeholder is preserved so placeholders can be concatenated with prefixes/suffixes or with each other. Section values override common values, while mappings present in both are merged recursively. The output is the fully resolved section as a sorted JSON object.

input.config:
common:
  my_var: my_value
  my_file: "{{ file='include.yaml' }}"
  my_env_var: "{{ env='CUSTOM_ENV' }}"
test_dirs:
  dirs:
    - dst: /tmp/test_directory1/
  templates:
    - template: template1.yaml.j2

feature4_filters.json.description: Renders templates that exercise the engine's value-transformation filters and a dashed key lookup. A base64-encode filter encodes its input string, a base64-decode filter decodes its input, a sha256 filter returns the hex digest of its input, and a to-yaml filter serializes a structure (a list of mappings) to inline YAML. A separate template indexes a mapping by a region key to select the matching value. Output is the rendered file content under a '# file:' header.

input.config:
common:
  k8s_namespace: namespace
test_filters:
  to_base64: hello world
  from_base64: azhzLWhhbmRsZQ==
  to_sha256: k8s-hanle
  affinity:
    - key: "dedicated"
      operator: "Equal"
      value: "monitoring"
      effect: "NoSchedule"
    - key: "dedicated"
      operator: "Equal"
      value:
        hello: world
      effect: "NoSchedule"
  templates:
    - template: filters.yaml.j2

cases.expected_output:
# file: filters.yaml
b64encode: aGVsbG8gd29ybGQ=
b64decode: k8s-handle
sha256: 8fae6dd899aace000fd494fd6d795e26e2c85bf8e59d4262ef56b03dc91e924c
affinity: [{effect: NoSchedule, key: dedicated, operator: Equal, value: monitoring}, {effect: NoSchedule, key: dedicated, operator: Equal, value: {hello: world}}]

feature4_render_errors.json.description: Reports rendering failures as neutral error categories. Asking the engine to render from a null context is rejected with 'error=none_context'. A section that resolves with no usable template items prints 'error=no_template_items'. Referencing a template file that does not exist (including a name that is an unmatchable/invalid pattern, which is then treated as a literal name) prints 'error=template_not_found' with the missing template name. Rendering a template that references an undefined variable under strict-undefined semantics prints 'error=undefined_variable' with the variable name.

feature4_render_templates.json.description: Renders the templates declared by a resolved configuration section into output files, returning each produced file's relative path and rendered content. Template items may be listed under a 'templates' key or, equivalently, under a 'kubectl' key; items may be nested inside arbitrarily deep 'group' wrappers and are flattened; and a template name that is a regular expression is expanded to every available template file whose name it matches. Each context variable is available to the template engine, an include placeholder resolves to a nested mapping rendered with its native text form, a value piped through the base64-encode filter is encoded, a literal piped through the base64-decode filter is decoded, a static-file include global splices in the indented contents of matching files, and a list-files global yields the sorted base names of matching files. Output lists files in sorted path order, each introduced by a '# file:' header.

input.config:
common:
  my_var: my_value
  my_file: "{{ file='include.yaml' }}"
  my_env_var: "{{ env='CUSTOM_ENV' }}"
test_dirs:
  dirs:
    - dst: /tmp/test_directory1/
  templates:
    - template: template1.yaml.j2
    - template: template2.yaml.j2
    - template: template3.yaml.j2
    - template: template_include_file.yaml.j2
    - template: innerdir/template1.yaml.j2
    - template: template_list_files.yaml.j2

cases.expected_output:
# file: innerdir/template1.yaml
{'ha_ha': 'included_var'}
# file: template1.yaml
{'ha_ha': 'included_var'}
# file: template2.yaml
TXkgdmFsdWU=
# file: template3.yaml
My value
# file: template_include_file.yaml
test: |
  {{ hello world }}
  new
  line

  {{ hello world1 }}
# file: template_list_files.yaml
test: |
  template1.yaml.j2:
  my_file.txt:
  my_file1.txt:

feature5_evaluate_tags.json.description: Decides whether a template that carries a set of tags should be included given an optional allow-list ('only') and an optional skip-list ('skip'). A template is excluded when an allow-list is given and the template shares no tag with it, and excluded when a skip-list is given and the template shares any tag with it; otherwise it is included. When no lists are given the template is always included, and these rules also hold when the template has no tags at all. Each evaluation prints a 'match=true' or 'match=false' line in order.

cases.expected_output:
match=true
match=false
match=false
match=false
match=true
match=false
match=true

feature5_parse_tags.json.description: Normalizes the tags attached to a template item into a set. Tags provided as a list become the set of distinct entries, and tags provided as a single comma-separated string are split on commas into a set; a one-element list and a single bare string each yield a single-tag set. Supplying tags of any other type is rejected with 'error=unexpected_tags_type'. Each parsed result prints a 'tags=' line with the members in sorted order.

feature6_client_configuration.json.description: Assembles a Kubernetes client configuration from CLI, context, and environment inputs by precedence, requiring both a master URI and a token. A missing master URI is rejected with 'error=missing_master_uri' and a missing token with 'error=missing_token'. On success it reports the host (master URI), the bearer authorization header built from the token, whether debug is enabled (a debug flag supplied through the context as a truthy string turns it on), and the decoded certificate-authority text obtained by base64-decoding the supplied CA value.

feature6_env_deprecated.json.description: Detects whether legacy/deprecated environment variables are present for supplying the cluster master URI or certificate authority. The check is true when either deprecated variable holds a non-empty value and false when both are absent or empty. Each environment set prints a 'deprecated=true' or 'deprecated=false' line.

feature6_first_truthy.json.description: Picks the first 'truthy' value from an ordered argument list, modeling configuration precedence where higher-priority sources are listed first. Falsy values (none, false, zero, empty string, empty mapping, empty list) are skipped, and if every argument is falsy or there are no arguments the result is 'none'. Each argument set prints a 'first=' line with the selected value or 'none'.

feature6_namespace_default.json.description: Resolves the effective namespace by precedence: an explicit namespace from the configuration context wins over a namespace taken from a supplied kubeconfig default, which in turn wins over a namespace from the environment. When the context value is absent the kubeconfig default is used if provided, otherwise the environment value is used. Each scenario prints a 'namespace=' line with the resolved value or 'none'.

feature7_normalize_kind.json.description: Converts a Kubernetes resource kind written in CamelCase into a lower-cased, underscore-separated identifier, splitting at each capital letter so that a single-word kind becomes one lowercase word and a multi-word kind becomes underscore-joined words. Each input kind prints a 'name=' line with its normalized form.

feature8_deprecation.json.description: Decides whether a given apiVersion/kind pair is deprecated relative to a server version, using a deprecation table that maps each apiVersion and kind to a 'since' version and an optional 'until' version. The pair is not deprecated when the apiVersion or the kind is absent from the table, or when the server version is below 'since'. It is deprecated once the server version reaches or exceeds 'since', and also when an 'until' is set and the server version reaches or exceeds it (unsupported); an empty 'until' is ignored. Comparisons are semantic-version aware and boundary-inclusive. Each case prints the apiVersion, kind, server version, and a 'deprecated=' boolean.

feature9_resources_by_version.json.description: Returns the set of resource kinds a cluster catalog advertises for a queried API group/version. When the queried version matches the catalog's version, the advertised kinds are returned; when it does not match, the empty set is returned. Each query prints a 'group=... kinds=...' line with the kinds in sorted, comma-separated order (empty when none).

feature9_version_processable.json.description: Classifies whether an API group identifier matches a getter's version shape. A core getter accepts a bare single-segment version with no slash and rejects a slashed group/version, a lone slash, and an empty string. A regular getter is the mirror image: it accepts a group/version pair containing exactly one slash and rejects a bare version, a lone slash, and an empty string. Each query prints a line with the quoted input and a 'processable=' boolean.

cases.expected_output:
group='v1' processable=true
group='app/v1' processable=false
group='/' processable=false
group='' processable=false
